text: The video shows a laparoscopic cholecystectomy in an elderly patient with dense bowel adhesions from a previous laparotomy. The surgeon carefully releases the adhesions and punctures the gallbladder, revealing infected bile that is suctioned out. A tooth forceps is used to gently pull the gallbladder upward.

The surgeon makes an incision on the peritoneum near the gallbladder, carefully delineating the gallbladder wall and identifying the cystic duct. The incision is kept close to the gallbladder to ensure safety for the bile duct. Suction is used to reveal the duct, followed by bipolar cautery for hemostasis. A monopolar hook dissects the fat surrounding the cystic duct in the Hartmann's pouch region, gradually exposing the cystic duct structure and the lower ridge of the gallbladder.

The surgeon ensures hemostasis before continuing the incision on the anterior side towards the cystic plate and liver. Due to difficulty in holding the gallbladder, the assisting hand lifts it from below to improve traction. The surgeon uses suction for blunt dissection and to remove inflammatory fluid obstructing the view. The border of the gallbladder is identified, and short bursts of cautery are applied to strands close to the gallbladder, ensuring no major structures are affected.

The surgeon uses short bursts of cautery to dissect tissue parallel to the cystic artery and cystic duct, creating a wide window between the two structures. The cystic duct is encircled, and further dissection is performed to separate the lower border of the gallbladder from the liver bed, achieving a clear view of the tubular structures entering the gallbladder. On the posterior side, meticulous dissection removes peritoneum, revealing the gallbladder bed and cystic duct. As the surgeon attempts to delineate the friable cystic artery, it suddenly tears, causing a pulsatile bleed.

The surgeon suctions blood from the surgical field to improve visibility, revealing a single structure entering the gallbladder up to the liver bed. Two proximal clips are applied to secure the area. The gauze is removed to assess the bleeding, which has stopped after compression. The surgeon begins dissecting the gallbladder from the liver bed, carefully identifying the gallbladder wall and maintaining proximity to avoid entering the liver parenchyma.

The surgeon attempts to change the direction of dissection to better identify the anatomical planes between the gallbladder and liver bed. The peritoneum is incised up to the gallbladder fundus, revealing the thickened gallbladder wall. The assisting hand provides traction without grasping the gallbladder, aiding in the dissection process. The surgeon identifies an incorrect plane, inadvertently entering the gallbladder, and then adjusts to dissect between the liver and gallbladder wall to find the correct plane.

The surgeon uses a combination of sharp and blunt dissection to open the correct plane between the gallbladder and liver bed. The assistant provides counter traction by pulling the gallbladder towards their port, facilitating the identification of dissection planes. The peritoneum is gradually incised, and the gallbladder is released from surrounding tissue, with small vessels and tissue strands being carefully dissected to avoid entering the liver parenchyma.

The surgeon rotates the gallbladder from the left to the right side, releasing the last few attachments near the fundus. The dissection is completed, revealing the liver bed. Hemostasis is achieved using bipolar cautery, and the gallbladder is placed in an endo bag for retrieval.
